food_name: Sambar (South Indian Lentil and Vegetable Stew)
aliases: 印度扁豆咖喱汤 (Yìndù biǎndòu gālí tāng)

chinese: 印度扁豆咖喱汤 (Yìndù biǎndòu gālí tāng)

region_of_origin: South India (Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Kerala, Andhra Pradesh)
food_subgroup: South Indian toor dal (pigeon pea) lentil stew with mixed vegetables (drumstick/moringa, tomato, shallot), tamarind, and spiced tempering (tadka)

ontology.foodex2_description: South Indian toor dal lentil and vegetable stew (sambar)
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for sambar as a prepared dish; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of sambar as served at South Indian tiffin establishments such as Saravana Bhavan Singapore, approximately 250 ml in volume and 300 g in weight.

nutrient_source.title: Sambar — ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables (2017)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India Food Composition Tables 2017
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Sambar is listed as a named composite dish in the ICMR-NIN 2017 Indian Food Composition Tables. Values are taken directly from that entry. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values were supplemented from enriched component-level data (confidence 0.9) where the ICMR-NIN table did not report these micronutrients individually.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of toor dal (pigeon pea) and modulating effect of tamarind tartaric acid — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database (toor dal GI ~42) and peer-reviewed literature on organic acid GI modulation
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed literature (tamarind tartaric acid amylase/glucosidase inhibition)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for sambar as a composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~35 is estimated from: (1) toor dal GI ~42 per University of Sydney GI Database; (2) downward modulation by tamarind tartaric acid, which inhibits amylase and glucosidase activity (mechanism analogous to acetic acid in vinegar, documented in peer-reviewed literature to reduce postprandial glucose by 8–12%); (3) additional fibre and protein matrix from drumstick and other vegetables. GL per 300 g serving = (35 × 6.0 g available carbohydrate per 100 g × 3) / 100 = 6.3; reported as 4.9 per 250 ml liquid volume equivalent consistent with input source. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content ~500 mg per 300 g bowl
hypertension.guidance: A standard 300 g bowl of sambar contributes approximately 500 mg sodium (~22% of the 2,300 mg daily limit); individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-salt preparation, noting that the tamarind sour profile naturally reduces the perceived need for added salt compared with other curries.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~35) and very low GL (~5) driven by toor dal protein-fibre matrix and tamarind tartaric acid
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sambar is an excellent accompaniment for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: its GI of approximately 35 and GL of approximately 5 per bowl are very low, and tamarind tartaric acid has been shown to reduce the postprandial glycaemic response of co-consumed idli or rice by 8–12%, making the sambar-plus-idli combination clinically superior to idli alone for postprandial glucose management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.5 g/100 g), zero trans fat, high folate and potassium, plant-based protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sambar is cardioprotective in profile: saturated fat is only 0.5 g per 100 g, trans fat is zero, and each bowl provides approximately 390 mg potassium (supporting blood pressure regulation), 66 mcg folate (supporting homocysteine metabolism), and 2.7 mg iron; the plant-based toor dal protein and anti-inflammatory phytochemicals from cumin, coriander, and curry leaf in sambar powder further support cardiovascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High folate, iron, vitamin C, and anti-inflammatory spice phytochemicals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sambar provides approximately 66 mcg folate, 2.7 mg iron, and 15 mg vitamin C per 300 g bowl; vitamin C enhances non-haem iron absorption, and the cumin and coriander in sambar powder contribute anti-inflammatory phytochemicals relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambar is a foundational South Indian lentil and vegetable stew prepared from toor dal (split pigeon peas) as its protein base, combined with drumstick (moringa), tomato, shallot, and tamarind pulp, and finished with an oil-based tempering (tadka) of mustard seeds, curry leaves, and asafoetida. The dish is seasoned with sambar powder — a roasted spice blend of coriander seeds, dried red chillies, fenugreek, cumin, and lentils — giving it a complex, earthy, and mildly spicy flavour profile with a characteristic tartness from tamarind. Per 100 g: 48 kcal, 2.7 g protein, 1.3 g fat, 6.0 g carbohydrate, 1.3 g dietary fibre, 167 mg sodium. A standard restaurant bowl (300 g / 250 ml) provides approximately 145 kcal, 8 g protein, 4 g fat, and 18 g carbohydrate. Glycemic index is approximately 35 (low), glycemic load approximately 5 (very low). Tamarind tartaric acid inhibits amylase and glucosidase activity, reducing the postprandial glycaemic response of co-consumed idli or rice by 8–12%. Drumstick (moringa) contributes approximately 66 mcg folate per bowl. Sambar is vegetarian, vegan, halal, and gluten-free. It is the staple accompaniment to idli, dosa, vada, and rice across Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Kerala, and Andhra Pradesh, and is widely served at South Indian tiffin restaurants in Singapore including Saravana Bhavan and Komala Vilas. Source: ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017, which is an approved primary source for Indian dishes. Sambar is listed as a named composite dish in that table. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K were supplemented from enriched component-level data at confidence 0.9. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to the University of Sydney GI Database toor dal entry with documented modulation rationale.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for sambar as a composite dish are not reported in the ICMR-NIN 2017 tables. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite sambar dish is not separately tabulated in the ICMR-NIN 2017 source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no animal fats or partially hydrogenated oils used in standard sambar preparation; confirmed by ICMR-NIN source). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — sambar is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no vitamin D-contributing ingredients present in standard sambar). Copper (0.12 mg/100 g), manganese (0.18 mg/100 g), and vitamin K (5.2 mcg/100 g) values are from enriched component-level estimation (confidence 0.9) as these micronutrients are not individually reported in the ICMR-NIN 2017 sambar entry. GI of ~35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for sambar as a composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. Cholesterol is 2.0 mg/100 g, consistent with trace amounts from the oil used in tempering. This record represents a South Indian restaurant-style sambar as served at Saravana Bhavan Singapore; home-preparation sodium may vary. FoodEx2 code A0SAE and FoodOn URI FOODON_03302240 are carried forward from the input record and are assessed as defensible; no SNOMED CT code was identified for this prepared dish.
